title: Chapter 4 (expert) — Destructible terrain & fast maths
meta.desc: Expert level: heightfield generation (sum of sines, edge blend, platforms), craters as geometric subtraction via squared distance, caverns and overhangs, determinism, and turbo continuous wind (smoothstep + gust wave).
hero.title: Destructible terrain & fast maths
hero.lead: The deep end: generating a heightfield from a sum of sines, carving craters as geometric subtraction, building caverns from a one-line solidity rule, and the continuous turbo wind — all with cheap, deterministic maths.
s1.h2: Heightfield generation: a sum of sines
s1.lead: The surface is one height per column, built by adding three sine waves of decreasing amplitude and increasing frequency — large hills, then bumps, then fine detail. A seeded RNG picks the amplitudes, frequencies and phases.
s1.p1: Two refinements make it playable: the ends are forced to flat tower platforms, and an edgeBlend factor ramps the relief in from those platforms so hills never start with a cliff at the towers.
s1.callout.nerd: For the curious. The whole authoritative simulation is square-root-free: trajectories in additive steps, tower hits via axis-aligned boxes, craters via squared distance. The one surviving Math.sqrt serves feel (the proximity whizz), not logic — the full story is in the maths annex.
s2.p1: Solidity is a single predicate: below the surface and in no crater. Because a crater can sit fully under a bump, the matter above stays solid — so caverns and overhangs emerge for free, no special case.
s2.p2: The shell collision uses exactly this: a projectile dies and carves a crater the instant it reaches a solid point — and the same rule lets a later shell fly through the hollow it left behind.
s2.xsec.title: Solid minus craters
s2.xsec.cap: A crater under the ridge leaves an overhang above it: the same simple test produces a rich topology.
s3.p1: A crater is not painted into a bitmap. It's a circle subtracted from solid space, stored as { x, y, r }. “Is this point in a crater?” is the squared-distance test — monotonic, so no square root is needed.
s3.p2: Because the square root is monotonic, comparing dx² + dy² with r² gives exactly the same verdict as comparing the distance with r, using only multiplications. It's Pythagoras, stripped of its root.
s3.callout.nerd: Emergent geometry. No mesh, no boolean polygon ops. A height array plus a list of circles, queried by one boolean function, is enough to model Worms-style caves.
s4.h2: Determinism: the seed is the terrain
s4.p1: Generation is a pure function of the seed and the biome's roughness. The server rolls the seed, builds the heights, and ships only the seed — every client rebuilds the identical surface, so collisions resolved on the server land exactly where the TV drew the ground.
s4.code.c1: // fresh battlefield; only the seed crosses the wire
s4.p2: And the craters? They follow the same discipline: born from deterministic events (impacts resolved server-side) and travelling in the snapshot as a list. Same seed + same crater list ⇒ same terrain, bit for bit, everywhere.
s4.callout.tip: Same number, same world. The RNG (mulberry32) is seedable and deterministic. Banishing Math.random() from shared generation is what guarantees the heightfield can be reconstructed instead of transmitted.
s5.p1: Turbo wind never snaps. It eases between random keypoints every 10 seconds with a smoothstep polynomial, and a two-sine gust wave (scaled by the match's rolled-once gustiness) rides on top so the breeze breathes instead of gliding mechanically.
s5.p2: Keypoints are kept under 80% of the maximum so the gust has headroom before hitting the clamp — and the whole thing is just multiplications, additions and two sines: cheap enough to run every tick.
s5.callout.nerd: Remember. Batch 4 is fast, deterministic maths end to end: terrain as a sum of sines, craters as squared-distance subtraction, caves from one boolean, the seed as the wire format, and wind eased by a polynomial. For the deeper why, see the maths annex.
s6.lead: You've gone through Tower Duel all the way to the metal:
s6.p1: a deterministic loop, asset-free rendering, an authoritative protocol, and a destructible terrain held together by a handful of well-chosen formulas. Everywhere the same discipline: separate what changes from what computes, and prefer reproducible simplicity.
